sections.h:
Investigating Officer (applicant) checklist for non-disclosure applications

ALL non-disclosure applications must be heard by a magistrate sitting in chambers at the Perth Magistrates Court. (Magistrates Court (General) Rules 2005 regulation 46A.) Only the Chief or Deputy Chief Magistrate hear non-disclosure applications and only for matters that are currently before the Magistrates Court. Once a matter has been committed up to a superior court, another non-disclosure application will need to be made through the DPP.

For any applications for a non-disclosure order in the Children’s Court, contact the Perth Children’s Court DPP office for guidance on making the application.

The latest, up-to-date forms are available on the Prosecution Blue Book site (Prosecuting Library > Prosecuting Forms > Non-Disclosure Documents) and can also be accessed through the Prosecution System (Additional Forms).

• Contact the nominated non-disclosure prosecutor at Prosecution Standards And

Capability (phone: 6372 6331 or 6372 6332 – internal extensions 16331 or 16332).

▪ This preliminary discussion and review will help establish the proper basis for the application or

reason why the application should not go ahead.

• Complete the ‘Non-Disclosure Application - Police’.

 Fill in the details re accused, charge/s and investigating officer;

▪ Please use full charge/court no e.g. MC PE 2023/0000;

▪ Charges can be grouped; e.g. MC PP 2023/1234-1240;

 Metro applicants - DO NOT SIGN the application – it will be signed at the application hearing.

 Country applicants - sign and date the form.

 Complete Part 1 sections A and B;

▪ If necessary, uncheck the ‘yes’ box and check the ‘no’ box as required;

▪ Dates will be entered by the nominated non-disclosure prosecutor;

 Complete Part 2;

 If necessary, uncheck the current boxes and check the appropriate box as required - complete the text area of other if applicable;

 Complete Part 3;

 Part 4 will be completed by the nominated non-disclosure prosecutor.

• Draw up a confidential affidavit as per ‘Confidential Affidavit (Example)’.

 If cutting and pasting, please ensure all bold areas are corrected (unbolded);

 Metro applicants - DO NOT SIGN affidavit - you will be required to attend the application hearing and sign the affidavit in front of the magistrate;

 Country applicants - have affidavit sworn and signed by a JP (remember to sign the bottom of each page) unless attending Perth.

 The affidavit can cover multiple accused for the same ‘incident’.

• Complete the ‘Form 6 - Application In A Prosecution’.

 IMPORTANT NOTE: You will need a separate Form 6 for each accused. Also, if an accused has charges being dealt with in the summary jurisdiction and other charges to be dealt with in a superior jurisdiction, then that accused will need two Form 6s. One for each jurisdiction.

 Ensure the charge number/s are entered at the top of the form e.g. MC PE 2023/0000-0007;

 Complete accused’s details and applicant’s (police) details;

 Date will be entered by the nominated non-disclosure prosecutor unless otherwise advised;

 DO NOT LODGE the form - this will be done by the nominated non-disclosure prosecutor;

 Metro applicants will sign the form in front of the magistrate.

 Country applicants - sign and date the form but do not complete the dates for lodgement or hearing.

See also note * below.

• Complete relevant ‘Magistrate Order’.

 Please ensure that the relevant affidavit paragraph number is entered in the appropriate place.

 You will need the appropriate magistrate’s order for every Form 6 completed.

• Forward completed paperwork to the nominated non-disclosure prosecutor at

Prosecution Standards And Capability.

 Metro officers may email or hand-deliver paper work as they consider fit, based upon the sensitivity and security required.

 Country applicants are to arrange secure delivery (not email) due to paperwork having had to be signed prior;

▪ NOTE: - if country applicants intend to attend the application in person, they are to follow the metro

applicants’ checklist.

 *IMPORTANT NOTE: The original signed affidavit must be forwarded NOT a scanned copy. This procedure has been clarified with the Chief Magistrate.

• Metro applicants - attend Magistrates Court, Perth (Central Law Courts) as directed by the

nominated non-disclosure prosecutor.

• No firearms are to be worn when entering the magistrate’s chambers.

• Bring a pen!

sections.h:
CONFIDENTIAL AFFIDAVIT

NON-DISCLOSURE OF CERTAIN DOCUMENTS

The following example affidavit is a guide only and suggests some ways in which to compile your affidavit. The basic format suggested here is: - your details;

the details of the accused and what charges they are facing (one affidavit can cover more than one accused);

succinct background of the prosecution case – There is no need to go into specific minute detail. This background is so that the magistrate is aware of the general evidence available.

I, full name of officer, a rank of the Western Australia Police Force (WAPOL), attached to station / office, street address, suburb in the State of Western Australia, being duly swornaffirmed make oathaffirmation and say as follows:

I am an officer of the WAPOL.

The facts deposed to herein are true to the best of my knowledge, information and belief based on my direct personal knowledge and examination of records held by the WAPOL.

I am an investigating officer for a brief of evidence against full name of accused (accused’ surname). [You can then use just the surname – “Accused1” is used from here on as the example surname.] Accused1 is charged with offence/s, section/s (e.g. aggravated burglary with intent to commit an offence, s 401(1); 4x possess prohibited drug with intent to sell or supply, s 6(1), etc.)

BACKGROUND

Accused1 is a secondary target of Operation Orange, which is investigating the illicit activities of a group of persons involved in the importation and distribution of heroin or whatever. Operation Orange is on-going.

It will be alleged that on 01/01/2099, investigators (or police officers) from the name of section executed a (type, e.g. Misuse of Drugs Act) search warrant on Accused1’s residential address at full address. Accused1 was in possession of approximately 19 grams of heroin that was buried in his backyard or whatever the allegation is.

A registered informant has assisted police in obtaining telecommunication intercepts of the mobile telephones of the principal targets of this operation.

The same informant has also assisted a police undercover operative to successfully infiltrate the syndicate.

The undercover operative was instrumental in the arrest of Accused1 and will have evidence to give in regards to that matter and also to other matters concerning Accused1.

The undercover operative has established credibility in the syndicate and is endeavouring to gather evidence as to the identity of all the parties and the role each party plays in the syndicate.

MATERIAL THE SUBJECT OF THE PROPOSED ORDER

I therefore wish to make application to dispense with the disclosure :

the name of the informant;

all documents on which information provided by the informant is recorded;

all documents relating to the identity of the informant;

the name of the undercover police operative;

all documents on which information provided by the undercover police operative is recorded;

all recordings made by the undercover operative by way of audio-tape;

the telecommunications interception warrant and the associated evidentiary certificates; and all Telephone Intercept product.

REASONS FOR DELAYED DISCLOSURE

The reasons for non-disclosure of this evidence is:-

to protect the safety of the informant; and

disclosure would prejudice on-going investigations.

Evidence from the undercover police operative and telephone intercept will be disclosed prior to Accused1 going to trial for the charges he is currently facing.

That same evidence will result in Accused1 facing more charges of a similar nature.

It is intended that the evidence will be disclosed to the accused prior to the trial. (if the intention is to not disclose the material at all, use the next paragraph wording. It may be the intent to disclose some materiel before trial and some not at all; therefore, use both wordings in relation to each lot of material.) It is intended that the evidence will not be disclosed to the accused. It is not intended for any evidence or information obtained by these means to form part of the prosecution case as it will not be led at trial.

I verily believe there is good reason for this application and no miscarriage of justice will result in the non-disclosure at this time.

SWORN/AFFIRMED AT PERTH IN THE )

STATE OF WESTERN AUSTRALIA )

THIS DAY OF (month) 20 )

BEFORE ME: …………………………………….

MAGISTRATE

INTRODUCTION

The purpose of this Manual is to not only provide investigators with guidance as to what the Criminal Procedure Act (CPA) requires but to also introduce procedures directed to addressing the problems associated with determining what material may assist the accused’s defence, use of the Prosecution System and non-disclosure orders.

INTRODUCTION The purpose of this Manual is to not only provide investigators with guidance as to what the Criminal Procedure Act (CPA) requires but to also introduce procedures directed to addressing the problems associated with determining what material may assist the accused’s defence, use of the Prosecution System and non-disclosure orders.

sections.h:
The disclosure process begins on day one of the investigation. In order to meet the obligation of disclosure, it is imperative that as the investigation progresses all of the material gathered is recorded and retained. If the investigator cannot identify and keep track of all the material, it is impossible to be certain that the obligation of disclosure has been met.

Keep an up-to-date list of all physical material collected, as well as all witness identified.

There is no question that disclosure is more manageable and less onerous if the following measures are adopted right from the commencement of the investigation.

Physical Material and Witnesses are now recorded in the Prosecution System under the case items, case documents and witness sections. Should an investigation progress to a charge the Prosecution System will then create a Unified Physical Material List (UPML) and Unified Witness List (UWL).

Creating a “case” in the Prosecution System should be the first investigative action taken to enable all evidentiary and all confessional material (with the exception of any audio video recording) to be electronically recorded and stored.

At the case level all material recorded is visible onscreen in the Prosecution System or by exporting (to excel) a list which has a description of the material capability auto populated from the Incident Management System (IMS) or by free text.

The creation of a case can be in relation to one investigation or alternatively a larger operation whereby all evidentiary and confessional material is gathered subsequent to multiple briefs being created against different accused for different offences.

If the investigation is large or complex, a decision should be made to appoint a disclosure officer. It will be the responsibility of the disclosure officer to assist the investigators to identify relevant material with a view to preparing the documentation required to meet the obligation of disclosure. It is important to appreciate that disclosure officers are not investigators. Ultimate responsibility for decisions about disclosure rests with an investigator.

Where a person is charged the Prosecution System requires this material to be selected as brief documents which will eventually generate disclosure documents and receipts. This is only necessary if an offender is processed as a charged person and a brief created. Should a prima facie case not be established the case in the Prosecution System can be closed without charge and without creating a prosecution brief.

sections.h:
Initial disclosure is the provision of legislated documents and notices as soon as practicable after an accused is served a prosecution notice.

Whilst the Criminal Procedure Act 2004 (CPA) at section 35(4), (5) and (6) draws distinction between the initial disclosure for simple offences, prescribed simple offences and indictable offences, Western Australian Police has ensured compliance for all through provision to the accused of the following:

A. Statement of Material Facts.

In addition to detailing the allegation, the statement of material facts (accused copy) provides an approved notice that the accused does or does not have a criminal record and an approved notice of the existence or non-existence of any confessional material.

B. Copy Prosecution Notice (combined summons or court hearing notice if applicable).

C. Copy of the accused criminal record.

D. Endorsed copy of any approved evidence matrix.

E. Any prescribed documents (currently there are no prescribed documents).

Initial disclosure must be served prior to a brief being set as “approved for court”.

The Prosecution System enforces service details as a required entry before a brief can be set to ‘approved for court’ thereby the provisions of section 35(9) and 35(10) CPA detailing provision whereby “it is impractical to do so” should not become an issue for the investigator.

The Evidence Matrix

Where an evidence matrix prepared for simple or either way offences is completed verbally between an investigator and the supervisor there is no recording of the evidence discussed and nothing in writing to disclose.

The WA Police has adopted a policy of making it available at an early stage with a view to encouraging an accused to enter an early plea. Many accused are reluctant to plead guilty until sufficiently aware of the case against them.

Additional Information

• As soon as practicable after an accused is served with notice:

a) of the existence of confessional material; or

b) that the accused has a criminal record,

the prosecutor must make available a copy of the material or record to the accused or the accused’s lawyer1.

• If before or at the accused’s first appearance, the accused requests a copy of his/her

criminal record, the prosecutor must, if practicable, obey the request before or at the first appearance2.

1 Criminal Procedures Act 2004 (WA), s35 (11)

2 Criminal Procedures Act 2004 (WA), s35 (12)

sections.h:
The duty to disclose material arises by operation of law and therefore arises whether or not the defence requests disclosure of particular material.

The obligation imposed on the prosecutor to serve an accused with all “confessional material” and “evidentiary material” relevant to the charge is known as Full Disclosure. Full Disclosure may vary depending on whether the charge is an indictable offence, an either way charge to be tried on indictment or summarily, a listed simple offence, or a simple offence.

For the purposes of Full Disclosure, regardless of whether it is an indictable offence, an either way charge tried summarily, a listed simple offence, or a simple offence, the following definitions are applicable:

• Confessional material

• Evidentiary material

Charges where full disclosure is required or ordered in Magistrates Court (Trial) Where an accused pleads not guilty to an either way charge to be tried summarily or a listed simple offence, full disclosure is required to be served on the accused.3 Where an accused pleads not guilty to any other simple offence the court may order full disclosure to be served on the accused.4 In such cases the disclosure is to be affected as soon as is practicable after the adjournment where the accused pleaded not guilty and, in any event, at least 28 days before the trial date. In these instances, the prosecutorial authority remains the Western Australia Police Force as the conduct of the prosecution stays with the Magistrates Court.

The accused must be served full disclosure as follows:

A. any confessional material of the accused that is relevant to the charge and that the accused has not already received from the prosecutor;

B. any evidentiary material that is relevant to the charge;

C. a copy of the accused’s criminal record, if the accused has not already received it from the prosecutor;

D. any document that is prescribed (currently there are no prescribed documents).

Charges committed to a superior court

In the case of an indictable offence or an either way offence to be tried on indictment disclosure is to be made in one of two ways dependent upon whether they have entered a 3 Criminal Procedure Act 2004 section 61(5).

4 Criminal Procedure Act 2004 section 60(5) and section 61(5).

plea of guilty (for sentencing) or either been committed for a plea to be taken or alternatively having entered a not guilty plea.

Committed for sentencing

Within 21 days of an accused being committed for sentence the Office of the Director for Public Prosecutions must be given the following materials:

A. a copy of the written statement of material facts last served on the accused under s35 (when Initial Disclosure was provided);

B. any confessional material;

C. a copy of the accused’s criminal record; and

D. a certificate under s45(5) (ie confirming that there has been compliances with s35

CPA.

Committed where there is a plea of not guilty or no plea has been entered.

Commencing January 1 2018, investigating officers will have at least 10 weeks from the last court date to the committal hearing date to enable Police to comply with section 42 CPA (superior court full disclosure). This period is by agreement between the Chief Magistrate, the ODPP and the Western Australia Police Force as a trial for 12 months.

Section 42(5) CPA. As soon as practicable and within 10 weeks the accused must be served:

A. any confessional material of the accused that is relevant to the charge and that the accused has not already received from the prosecutor;

B. any evidentiary material that is relevant to the charge;

C. any other document that is prescribed (currently there are no prescribed documents).

Section 45(3) CPA. As soon as practicable and within 10 weeks the ODPP must be given:

A. a copy of the written statement of material facts last served on the accused under section 35; and

B. any confessional material of the accused that is relevant to the charge; and C. any evidentiary material that is relevant to the charge, including anything referred to in section 42(2).

I. if it is not practicable to copy a document or object, to serve a notice that describes it and states where and when it can be inspected;

II. if a copy of a statement or recording of a person is served — to also serve a copy of any statement or recording of the person that contains material that is inconsistent with that statement or recording;

III. to serve notice of the name and, if known, the address of any person from whom no statement or recording of the kind has been obtained but who the prosecutor thinks may be able to give evidence that may assist the accused’s defence and a description of the evidence concerned.

D. anything that has been served on the accused in addition to service of full disclosure as additional material comes to light.

E. a copy of the accused’s criminal record; and

F. a certificate under s45(5) (ie confirming that there has been compliances with s35

CPA.

The benefit of having scanned, uploaded and entered all evidentiary and confessional material into the prosecution case can now be drawn upon to create a unified physical material list (UPML) and a Unified witness list (UWL).
sections.b: The duty to disclose material arises by operation of law and therefore arises whether or not the defence requests disclosure of particular material. The obligation imposed on the prosecutor to serve an accused with all “confessional material” and “evidentiary material” relevant to the charge is known as Full Disclosure. Full Disclosure may vary depending on whether the charge is an indictable offence, an either way charge to be tried on indictment or summarily, a listed simple offence, or a simple offence. For the purposes of Full Disclosure, regardless of whether it is an indictable offence, an either way charge tried summarily, a listed simple offence, or a simple offence, the following definitions are applicable: • Confessional material • Evidentiary material Charges where full disclosure is required or ordered in Magistrates Court (Trial) Where an accused pleads not guilty to an either way charge to be tried summarily or a listed simple offence, full disclosure is required to be served on the accused.3 Where an accused pleads not guilty to any other simple offence the court may order full disclosure to be served on the accused.4 In such cases the disclosure is to be affected as soon as is practicable after the adjournment where the accused pleaded not guilty and, in any event, at least 28 days before the trial date. In these instances, the prosecutorial authority remains the Western Australia Police Force as the conduct of the prosecution stays with the Magistrates Court. The accused must be served full disclosure as follows: A. any confessional material of the accused that is relevant to the charge and that the accused has not already received from the prosecutor; B. any evidentiary material that is relevant to the charge; C. a copy of the accused’s criminal record, if the accused has not already received it from the prosecutor; D. any document that is prescribed (currently there are no prescribed documents). Charges committed to a superior court In the case of an indictable offence or an either way offence to be tried on indictment disclosure is to be made in one of two ways dependent upon whether they have entered a 3 Criminal Procedure Act 2004 section 61(5). 4 Criminal Procedure Act 2004 section 60(5) and section 61(5). plea of guilty (for sentencing) or either been committed for a plea to be taken or alternatively having entered a not guilty plea. Committed for sentencing Within 21 days of an accused being committed for sentence the Office of the Director for Public Prosecutions must be given the following materials: A. a copy of the written statement of material facts last served on the accused under s35 (when Initial Disclosure was provided); B. any confessional material; C. a copy of the accused’s criminal record; and D. a certificate under s45(5) (ie confirming that there has been compliances with s35 CPA. Committed where there is a plea of not guilty or no plea has been entered. Commencing January 1 2018, investigating officers will have at least 10 weeks from the last court date to the committal hearing date to enable Police to comply with section 42 CPA (superior court full disclosure). This period is by agreement between the Chief Magistrate, the ODPP and the Western Australia Police Force as a trial for 12 months. Section 42(5) CPA. As soon as practicable and within 10 weeks the accused must be served: A. any confessional material of the accused that is relevant to the charge and that the accused has not already received from the prosecutor; B. any evidentiary material that is relevant to the charge; C. any other document that is prescribed (currently there are no prescribed documents). Section 45(3) CPA. As soon as practicable and within 10 weeks the ODPP must be given: A. a copy of the written statement of material facts last served on the accused under section 35; and B. any confessional material of the accused that is relevant to the charge; and C. any evidentiary material that is relevant to the charge, including anything referred to in section 42(2). I. if it is not practicable to copy a document or object, to serve a notice that describes it and states where and when it can be inspected; II. if a copy of a statement or recording of a person is served — to also serve a copy of any statement or recording of the person that contains material that is inconsistent with that statement or recording; III. to serve notice of the name and, if known, the address of any person from whom no statement or recording of the kind has been obtained but who the prosecutor thinks may be able to give evidence that may assist the accused’s defence and a description of the evidence concerned. D. anything that has been served on the accused in addition to service of full disclosure as additional material comes to light. E. a copy of the accused’s criminal record; and F. a certificate under s45(5) (ie confirming that there has been compliances with s35 CPA. The benefit of having scanned, uploaded and entered all evidentiary and confessional material into the prosecution case can now be drawn upon to create a unified physical material list (UPML) and a Unified witness list (UWL).

sections.h:
Preparing for the Disclosure/Committal Hearing

Using the Prosecution System generated UPML and the UWL and having regard to the definitions set out above, all the documents or objects gathered in the course of the investigation need to be accounted for as either:

A. Recommended exhibit

B. Other disclosed material

C. Unused material

D. Sensitive Material; or

E. Non-disclosure order.

The UPML as generated by the Prosecution System allows the case members to categorise physical materials as a recommended exhibit, other disclosed material, unused material or sensitive material.

All five categories are to be provided to the ODPP. Only categories A, B and C and D are to be provided to an accused.

By selecting the documents of the same category in the Unified Physical Material list in the Prosecution System, a UPML disclosure including proof of service document can be created for each category type.

Material subject to a non-disclosure order is not to be added to a prosecution brief from the case as it will be accounted for in any non-disclosure order, not on a UPML or UWL.

A. Recommended exhibit

This consists of all “confessional material”; and all “evidentiary material” (including “every document or object that may assist the accused’s defence”5) relevant to the charge.

B. Other disclosed material

This consists of material addition to that tending to prove or disprove a charge, for example where there is video footage of 1 hour recorded albeit only 5 minutes (recommended material) captures the incident in question.

C. Unused Material

This consists of all of the material gathered in the course of the investigation that is not “confessional” or “evidentiary” material (ie material that is not relevant to the charge and will neither assist the prosecution nor the accused that is not sensitive).

5 Mansell v State of Western Australia [2011] WASC 170 at [19] per McKechnie J.

The fact that the accused is to be provided with a list of Unused Material is not a substitute for ensuring that all “confessional” and “evidentiary” material has been given to the accused.

Given there may be a large body of material that falls into this category, where appropriate, a generic description of the material can be provided (eg box of accused’s bank statement 2010).

As there is no legal obligation to provide access to the unused material, the accused is not entitled to a copy. However, the accused (or his/her legal representative) will be permitted to inspect the unused material.

If the accused determines that the material falls within the definition of “confessional” or “evidentiary” material (and debatably should have been provided as recommended material) a copy should be provided. The fact that the accused requests a copy of the material does not of itself mean that the documents may assist the accused.

Where an accused is provided with a copy of material categorised as Unused Material, the investigator is to ensure that the ODPP is provided with a copy of whatever material is provided to the accused.

D. Sensitive Material

Sensitive material is material subject to:

(a) the Evidence Act 1906 sections 19C and 106HB (3);

(b) any other written law that relates to the disclosure of specific material;

(c) the law of privilege (i.e., legal professional privilege) including:

• Advice from the ODPP

• Legal professional privilege attaches to a confidential communication that was

made or brought into existence for the dominant purpose of:

I. obtaining legal advice; or

II. use in legal proceedings

• Legal professional privilege capable of applying to communications with the

ODPP that meet the above criteria).; or

(d) the law on public interest immunity (see s137A CPA) including:

• Material that discloses some unusual form of surveillance or method of

detecting crime. This extends to information that would compromise the ability of the police to investigate crime (i.e. if criminals had access to this information, they could avoid detection). It does not extend to information concerning police methodology that is well known or publicly available.

• Information concerning ongoing investigations

• Material, if it became known, that might facilitate the commission of other

offences.

• Material supplied to the police only on condition that it will not be disclosed (eg

Crime Stoppers).

Sensitive material is exempt from the duty of disclosure (see sections 137A & 138 CPA).

The material categorised as Sensitive Material should contain a brief description but must not disclose the content unless the court orders the content disclosed (ie there could be a reference to police methodology without disclosing what that methodology involves).

Where the mere disclosure (even in general terms) of the existence of the material is likely to disclose the content of the information (eg identify an informant), no reference is to be made to the material in the UPML. In this instance, a non-disclosure order is to be sought.

Non-disclosure orders are not to be itemised or referred to in any UPML.

Redacting Documents (part of which are sensitive)

Ordinarily the sensitive material is to be redacted (i.e. blocked out). The redacted document is to be included as a recommended exhibit, other disclosed material or will be available for inspection having been itemised as Unused Material. The original unredacted document is to be categorised as sensitive material so that its contents are not disclosed.

Where the redaction of sensitive material would:

(a) result in a substantial portion of the document being redacted; and

(b) the redaction of a substantial portion of the document would of itself, having regard to

the circumstances of the case, compromise the secrecy of the sensitive material (eg the redaction of multiple pages of a running sheet that predate the accused’s arrest would invariably disclose that there was a pre-existing broader investigation into other targets), a non-disclosure order should be sought for the entire document and a new document created that includes only the material to be disclosed.

Notwithstanding the fact that material has been itemised in the Schedule of Sensitive Material or is the subject of a non-disclosure order, if a court ultimately orders the production of the material, it is to be provided.

E. Non-Disclosure Orders

A non-disclosure order is an order for the non-disclosure of material made by a court pursuant to section 138(3) CPA. The order relieves the prosecution from having to comply with the legal requirement to disclose specific evidentiary material as described in the confidential application supplied to the court.

Applications for non-disclosure are made without notice to the accused and the application itself is dealt with in the absence of the accused such that the accused is unaware and does not have an opportunity to be heard.

An investigating officer intending to make application for non-disclosure must ensure there is good reason to do so and no miscarriage of justice will result.

A non-disclosure application can relate to evidentiary material which is never intended to be disclosed at any time or evidentiary material which is intended to be revealed, however, a delay is sought for disclosure to be given in order to protect persons from harm or to avoid interference with an ongoing investigation.

What cannot be revealed is the fact that a non-disclosure order exists. The Criminal Procedure Act 2004 section 138(5) specifically states the order must not be given or disclosed to the accused without the permission of the court that made the order.

Should the accused, however, discover the existence of the evidentiary material, they can obtain a summons seeking the material be disclosed. Subsequently, there would be an open court hearing to argue public interest immunity. At this hearing, the court will make a determination and order as to the disclosure of the evidentiary materials.

A non-disclosure order should be obtained where the mere itemisation, even in general terms, of sensitive material would compromise the secrecy of the information.

Copies of all non-disclosure orders are to be provided to the ODPP.

The non-disclosure order “must not be given or disclosed to the accused without the permission of the court that made it or of a superior court.

Where a non-disclosure order is required for any matter where the accused is currently appearing in the Magistrates Court; whether the matter is to be dealt with summarily or later on indictment; the application for a non-disclosure order is to be “heard by a magistrate sitting in chambers at the Court sitting at Perth.”6

These applications are made through a dedicated police prosecutor at Perth Prosecuting.

For matters which are to be committed up to a superior court, the non-disclosure order will only last until 42 days after the date upon which the matter is committed.

In the event that a further non-disclosure order is required after the disclosure/committal hearing, only the ODPP can make the application. The ODPP will consider the making of an application having regard to the supporting information and materials provided by the Western Australia Police Force.

6 Magistrates Court (General) Rules 2005 rule 46A.

sections.h:
Unified physical material list – adding documents from a case.

Once documents and other materials are added to the case items they can be selected to add to a brief UPML.

In the Brief vertical menu select UPML then select ‘Add from case list ’

Adding from the case list displays all available material from where a case officer can select all disclosable material to import to the UPML.

Individual materials in the UPML can then be categorised as a Recommended Exhibit, Other Disclosed Material, Unused Material or Sensitive Material and a description of each material added.

Unified Witness List – adding witnesses from Case List

In order to generate a UWL for disclosure purposes the listed witnesses in the case need to be selected and added to the UWL in the brief. This is achieved by the following steps:

Select UWL from the brief vertical menu.

Select add from case list.

A screen will display all available witnesses listed on the case, select the witnesses for the UWL and then select save.

Edit the details of the witness by selecting the pencil icon in the relevant witness line and select save.

Should the details of the witness not wish to be disclosed subject to a non-disclosure application, select ‘yes’ for Has Non-Disclosure Application and select save.

This witness will be flagged NDA and will not be able to appear on the UWL for future disclosure events.

Unified Physical Material List – Disclosure

To create a hard copy UPLM listing all material for disclosure, select UPML from the brief vertical menu. From this screen a case officer can select the order of and category of materials to be included in one or multiple disclosure lists.

Then select UPML Disclosure

Enter disclosure recipient details either defence or internal (Prosecution - ODPP), free type the disclosure recipient and select Generate

Generating a UPML will create a pdf document listing all items or only the selected category of disclosable material. A copy of this UPML is automatically saved in the case documents.

This document can be printed and used as a receipt requiring a recipient’s signature. Should you wish to include disclosable documents in this print task with the UPML select include documents.

Unified Witness List – Disclosure

Select UWL from the brief vertical menu and then select UWL Disclosure Enter disclosure recipient details either defence or internal (Prosecution - ODPP), free type the disclosure recipient details and select Generate

The UWL will be generated detailing the name, contact details, the role, whether a statement has been obtained, recommended or otherwise as a witness and a summary of their evidence in pdf form. A copy of this UWL is automatically included in the case documents.

Recording the Disclosure event

Officers are guided by the preference of recipients as to whether materials are to be served electronically or in hard copy. The Prosecution System stores all relevant documents for disclosure which can be printed to enable disclosure. Provided all documents are uploaded to the prosecution brief they and that the final brief is compiled, then the Prosecution System will provide both a single pdf document containing all material and a zip file which can be burnt to a disc or stored in a drive.

Regardless of the method of transfer, the disclosure event should be recorded in the Prosecution System. This will enable Police to demonstrate what was disclosed, to whom and on what date.

The UPML and the UWL lists should be signed by the serving member and by the recipient.

The signed documents need to be scanned and uploaded to the prosecution system as a disclosure receipt.

To record the disclosure event select Disclosure from the brief vertical menu identify the disclosure UPML or UWL served then select edit event.

Complete the disclosure details fields and select the link icon to upload the signed disclosure receipt from the case documents then select save.

The Disclosure of the example UWL now displays as Disclosed in the Prosecution System Non-Disclosure Order

An investigating officer intending to make application for non-disclosure must ensure there is good reason to do so and no miscarriage of justice will result.

A non-disclosure application can relate to evidentiary material which is never intended to be disclosed at any time or evidentiary material which is intended to be revealed however a delay is sought for disclosure to be given in order to protect persons from harm.

Applications for matters that are appearing in the Magistrates Court are made in the absence of the defence. All applications, for all matters state-wide, are made in Perth and any order that is granted will be granted by the Chief or Deputy Chief Magistrate.

Any order will only relieve the requirements under sections 35, 42 or 61. These will, generally, be for summary trials and matters going to a disclosure/committal hearing.

These applications are dealt with by a dedicated prosecutor at Perth Prosecuting. It is recommended investigating officers contact this Prosecutor for assistance and review the basis for the application. Contact telephone 089 218 5239.

Once a matter has been committed up to the District or Supreme Court, the DPP will need to make another application before that court to be relieved of any requirement under s95 of the CPA. These orders are granted in those courts.

Non-disclosure for Children's Court matters should be referred to the Children's Court DPP team at Perth Children's Court.

Where an investigating officer intends to make application for a non-disclosure order some applicable forms are located in the Prosecution System itself.

Select Forms and Printing from the vertical menu and look in the additional forms tab to find non-Disclosure which should then be selected.

There are 4 forms relevant to a non-disclosure application which can separately be selected for downloading. Select each one consecutively and download as a word document.

The only document required in addition to the above 4 will be a confidential affidavit.

A sample of this document which can be adjusted to suit individual needs can be located in the Prosecuting Division Blue Book site. This site contains the affidavit, other relevant documents including the above and other information relating to non-disclosure applications.

Complete the forms as follows:

1. Non-Disclosure application.

2. Draft the confidential affidavit, DO NOT SIGN the affidavit. The investigating officer

will be required to attend the application hearing and sign the affidavit before the Magistrate.

3. Complete the ‘Form 6 application in a prosecution’. DO NOT LODGE the form 6 with

the court, this will be done by the nominated Senior Prosecutor at Perth Prosecuting.

4. Complete ‘Magistrates Order’ entering the correct paragraph number referred to in

the affidavit.

The completed forms should be delivered or emailed to the identified Senior Prosecutor at Perth Prosecuting as determined by the sensitivity of the matter.

The investigating officer should be prepared to attend Perth Magistrates Court as directed by the Senior Prosecutor.

After the non-disclosure application has been determined, the associated documents and order are not to be uploaded to the Prosecution System brief. Whilst the ODPP needs to be aware of the non-disclosure action, the investigating officer is to be guided by the ODPP as to whether that body should retain any documents relating to non-disclosure. At the minimum copies should be retained in the hard copy crime file by the investigator.

MISCELLANEOUS

Disclosure Certificate – Section 45(5) CPA

Where the investigating officer is required to give disclosure to the ODPP in circumstances where a charge is committed to a superior court for sentencing, where no plea is entered or where a plea of not guilty is entered, they are required to certify compliance with section 35 and section 42 (as relevant).

The Disclosure Certificate under section 45(5) CPA is not a form found within the Prosecution System at this time. It is located in the intranet Western Australia Police investigative practices web site as a word document.

This form is to be completed and uploaded into the Prosecution System as a cover sheet.

When the final disclosure brief is recompiled, this document will then be at the top of the Prosecution brief.

A person who knowingly or without reasonable diligence signs a certificate under s45 (5) that is false in a material particular commits and offence for which the penalty is a fine of up to $5,000.

Confessional Material

For the purposes of initial and full disclosure (whether it be an indictable offence, a prescribed simple offence or a simple offence), “Confessional material” means:

Initial disclosure - section 35(1) CPA

a) any written statement signed by the accused;

b) any written record of interview with the accused (signed or unsigned by the accused);

and

c) any interview that has been electronically recorded (see s115 Criminal Investigation Act 2006)

Full disclosure - the above and further below under section 42(1) CPA d) a copy of any electronic recording of a conversation with the accused that is relevant to the charge other than a recording already provided as part of Initial Disclosure;

e) if the accused has said anything relevant to the charge that is not recorded, a written version of the substance of what was said.

that is relevant to the charge and that is in the possession of the WA Police.

See section 35(1) CPA and section 42(1) CPA.

Evidentiary Material

a) A copy of

i. every statement made in accordance with Schedule 3 clause 4 CPA ii. every recording that has been made in accordance with Schedule 3 clause 6

CPA

iii. every recording that has been made under the Evidence Act 1906 of: and iv. every other recorded statement, whether oral or written, by any person who may be able to give evidence that is relevant to the charge, irrespective of whether or not it assists the prosecution case, or the accused’s defence.

b) if there is no statement or recording referred to in paragraph (a) of a person who the prosecutor intends to call as a witness, a written summary of the evidence to be given by the person;

c) a copy of any document or object to which a statement or recording referred to in paragraph (a) refers:

d) a copy of every other document or object that the prosecutor intends to tender in evidence at trial; and

e) a copy of “every other document or object that may assist the accused’s defence”, that is in the possession of the organisation or person who investigated the offence.

A requirement to provide serve “evidentiary material” also includes a requirement:

1) if it is not practicable to copy a document or object (pursuant to in s42 (1) (c), (d) or

(e) of the definition of evidentiary material) – to serve a notice that describes it and

states where and when it can be inspected; (The fact that a large number of documents have been gathered in the course of the investigation does not mean, it is not practicable to copy the documents. It is confined to circumstances where it is not practicable to copy a particular document (i.e. a large map).

2) If a copy of a statement or recording of a person is served – to also provide a copy of

any statement or recording of the person that is inconsistent with the statement or recording;

3) to serve notice of the name and address of any person from whom no statement or

recording was taken but who the prosecutor thinks may be able to assist the accused’s defence and a description of the evidence concerned.

Material may assist the accused if the material can be seen on a sensible appraisal by the prosecution:

a) To be relevant or possibly relevant to an issue in the case b) To raise or possibly raise a new issue whose existence is not apparent from the evidence the prosecution proposes to use or

c) To hold out a real (as opposed to fanciful) prospect of providing a lead on evidence which goes to (a) or (b).

If there is doubt as to whether the material ought to be disclosed and the material is not sensitive, the material should be disclosed.

The following documents are assumed to assist the accused’s defence, and should be disclosed as a matter of course (unless sensitive):

• running sheets

• the investigative actions register

• investigators note

• decision logs

• broadcasts (i.e., BOLO)

• identikit images

• digi boards

• operational orders

Defence Disclosure

Defence disclosure relates to the intent by defence to introduce the following:

“alibi evidence”, in respect of an accused charged with an offence, means any evidence that tends to show that the accused was not present when the offence is alleged to have been committed.

“expert evidence material” relevant to a charge, means – a. a copy of every statement, recording or report obtained by the accused of any person who the accused intends to call to give expert evidence that is relevant to the charge;

b. written notice of the name and, if known, the address of any person from whom no statement, recording or report has been obtained by the accused but who the accused intends to call to give expert evidence that is relevant to the charge; and

c. a written description of the expert evidence referred to in paragraph (b) See s62(1) CPA

Consequences of Non-Disclosure

Charges to be dealt with on Indictment

If before or at a trial on indictment the court is satisfied that a party has not obeyed a disclosure requirement, the court on the application of the party affected by the breach, may adjourn the trial for a period that allows enough time –

(a) if necessary, for a party in breach of the requirement to obey it; and

(b) for a part affected by the breach to investigate properly any evidence or other matter

disclosed in accordance with the requirement and to obtain any evidence that may be necessary as a result of the disclosure, or, if the trial is a trial by jury, may discontinue the trial, discharge the jury from giving its verdict and adjourn the prosecution.

On any resumption of a trial adjourned as a consequence of non-disclosure, a party affected by the breach –

(a) may require a person who has given evidence in the trial, including the accused,

to be recalled as a witness;

(b) may cross-examine or further cross-examine the person about the evidence or

other matter disclosed in accordance with the disclosure requirement; and

(c) may adduce evidence in rebuttal of the evidence or other matter disclosed in

accordance with the disclosure requirements.

The failure by a party to obey a disclosure requirement may be the subject of adverse comment to the jury by the judge, the accused or the prosecutor.

Either Way Offences Tried Summarily, Listed Simple Offence or Simple Offence where the court has ordered the prosecution provide full disclosure or the accused provide Defence Disclosure.

If at the trial in a case the court is satisfied that a party has not obeyed a disclosure requirement, the court, on the application of a party affected by the breach, may adjourn the trial to a date that allows enough time –

(a) if necessary, for the party in breach of the requirement to obey it; and

(b) for a party affected by the breach to investigate properly any evidence or other

matter disclosed in accordance with the requirement and to obtain any further evidence that may be necessary as a result of the disclosure.

On the resumption of a trial that is adjourned under subsection (2) a party affected by the breach –

(a) may require a person who has given evidence, including the accused, to be

recalled as a witness;

(b) may cross-examine or further cross-examine the person about the evidence or

other matter disclosed in accordance with the disclosure requirement; and

(c) may adduce evidence in rebuttal of the evidence or other matter disclosed in

accordance with the disclosure requirement.

If a party does not obey a disclosure requirement, the court, in making an order for costs under section 67 or the Official Prosecutions (Accused’s Costs) Act 1973 in favour of the party, may reduce the amount that would otherwise be ordered by the amount of the costs that a party affected by the breach incurred as a result of the breach.

Common Categories of Documents and Objects

Forensic UPML

Forensic officers typically manage physical materials gathered during the course of an investigation separate to those physical materials recorded as property items in the Incident Management System. In order to account for those forensic materials the use of a Forensic UPLM has been adopted separate to the brief UPML.

Within the Prosecution System used to generate full disclosure there is the one UPML (for different categories as needed). The Forensic Report/register/UPML should be recorded as a document on the brief UPML as any other evidentiary report.

Intercepted or recorded conversations

Intercepted or recorded conversations obtained as part of the investigation, whether they be telephone calls or surveillance devices material in which the accused is a participant, are to be disclosed. However, in the case of a conspiracy or a joint criminal enterprise all intercepted or recorded conversations involving all alleged parties to the conspiracy or joint enterprise are to be provided.

Material in the possession of third parties

Where an investigator is aware of material that may assist an accused’s defence that is in the possession of a third party, the accused is to be informed of:

1. the name of the third party;

2. the nature of the material; and

3. the address of the third parties (unless there is good reason for not doing so, and if

so, it may be necessary for the prosecutor to facilitate communications between the accused and the third party).

Expert evidence

Where a report from an expert is obtained, not only is the report itself disclosable but so are the instructions provided to the expert and any requests for clarification. An accused is entitled to know what information was provided to the expert and the parameters of the request for advice. The results of experiments and tests carried out or know of by an expert which casts doubt on opinion expressed by him or her are also to be disclosed.

Material that undermines the defence case

The duty of disclosure does not require the disclosure of material that might undermine the defence case, such as matters affecting the credibility of defence witnesses; the defence is responsible for making its own inquiries in this regard.

Specific Operating Procedures

This disclosure manual provides the basic framework for all investigators to enable compliance with the CPA in relation to disclosure obligations.

Larger specialist units such as Major Crime Squad and Organised Crime Squad are able to apply resources and expertise focusing on the disclosure requirements to meet what is a core function and by-product of dealing with largely solely indictable offences.

These specialist investigative units have introduced separate standard operating procedures in partnership with and including an early engagement with the ODPP to enable uniformity and compliance with disclosure demands.

These local procedures include the creation of brief management teams, the appointment of a disclosure officer, Disclosure continuity forms, an early partial disclosure event to the ODPP six weeks after charge and other fit for purpose concepts that assist to manage a large amount of disclosable material gathered during compound and complex investigations.

Regardless of these specialist investigative units adopting local procedures, the Unified Crime Management System is decommissioned. The Prosecution System is now the case management storage system from which evidentiary material can be retrieved for inclusion in a Prosecution Brief and from which disclosure is generated.

The Prosecution System stores all relevant documents for disclosure which can be printed to enable disclosure. Regardless of the method of transfer, the disclosure event should be recorded in the Prosecution System. This will enable Police to demonstrate what was disclosed, to whom and on what date. The UPML and the UWL lists should be signed by the serving member and by the recipient. The signed documents need to be scanned and uploaded to the prosecution system as a disclosure receipt. To record the disclosure event select Disclosure from the brief vertical menu identify the disclosure UPML or UWL served then select edit event. Complete the disclosure details fields and select the link icon to upload the signed disclosure receipt from the case documents then select save. A non-disclosure application can relate to evidentiary material which is never intended to be disclosed at any time or evidentiary material which is intended to be revealed however a delay is sought for disclosure to be given in order to protect persons from harm. Applications for matters that are appearing in the Magistrates Court are made in the absence of the defence. All applications, for all matters state-wide, are made in Perth and any order that is granted will be granted by the Chief or Deputy Chief Magistrate. Any order will only relieve the requirements under sections 35, 42 or 61. These will, generally, be for summary trials and matters going to a disclosure/committal hearing. These applications are dealt with by a dedicated prosecutor at Perth Prosecuting. It is recommended investigating officers contact this Prosecutor for assistance and review the basis for the application. Contact telephone 089 218 5239. Once a matter has been committed up to the District or Supreme Court, the DPP will need to make another application before that court to be relieved of any requirement under s95 of the CPA. These orders are granted in those courts. Non-disclosure for Children's Court matters should be referred to the Children's Court DPP team at Perth Children's Court. Where an investigating officer intends to make application for a non-disclosure order some applicable forms are located in the Prosecution System itself. Select Forms and Printing from the vertical menu and look in the additional forms tab to find non-Disclosure which should then be selected. There are 4 forms relevant to a non-disclosure application which can separately be selected for downloading. Select each one consecutively and download as a word document. The only document required in addition to the above 4 will be a confidential affidavit. A sample of this document which can be adjusted to suit individual needs can be located in the Prosecuting Division Blue Book site. This site contains the affidavit, other relevant documents including the above and other information relating to non-disclosure applications. Complete the forms as follows: 1. Non-Disclosure application. 2. Draft the confidential affidavit, DO NOT SIGN the affidavit. The investigating officer will be required to attend the application hearing and sign the affidavit before the Magistrate. 3. Complete the ‘Form 6 application in a prosecution’. DO NOT LODGE the form 6 with the court, this will be done by the nominated Senior Prosecutor at Perth Prosecuting. 4. The Disclosure Certificate under section 45(5) CPA is not a form found within the Prosecution System at this time. It is located in the intranet Western Australia Police investigative practices web site as a word document. This form is to be completed and uploaded into the Prosecution System as a cover sheet. When the final disclosure brief is recompiled, this document will then be at the top of the Prosecution brief. A person who knowingly or without reasonable diligence signs a certificate under s45 (5) that is false in a material particular commits and offence for which the penalty is a fine of up to $5,000. Confessional Material For the purposes of initial and full disclosure (whether it be an indictable offence, a prescribed simple offence or a simple offence), “Confessional material” means: Initial disclosure - section 35(1) CPA a) any written statement signed by the accused; b) any written record of interview with the accused (signed or unsigned by the accused); and c) any interview that has been electronically recorded (see s115 Criminal Investigation Act 2006) Full disclosure - the above and further below under section 42(1) CPA d) a copy of any electronic recording of a conversation with the accused that is relevant to the charge other than a recording already provided as part of Initial Disclosure; e) if the accused has said anything relevant to the charge that is not recorded, a written version of the substance of what was said. that is relevant to the charge and that is in the possession of the WA Police. See section 35(1) CPA and section 42(1) CPA. Evidentiary Material a) A copy of i. every statement made in accordance with Schedule 3 clause 4 CPA ii. every recording that has been made in accordance with Schedule 3 clause 6 CPA iii. every recording that has been made under the Evidence Act 1906 of: and iv. every other recorded statement, whether oral or written, by any person who may be able to give evidence that is relevant to the charge, irrespective of whether or not it assists the prosecution case, or the accused’s defence. b) if there is no statement or recording referred to in paragraph (a) of a person who the prosecutor intends to call as a witness, a written summary of the evidence to be given by the person; c) a copy of any document or object to which a statement or recording referred to in paragraph (a) refers: d) a copy of every other document or object that the prosecutor intends to tender in evidence at trial; and e) a copy of “every other document or object that may assist the accused’s defence”, that is in the possession of the organisation or person who investigated the offence. A requirement to provide serve “evidentiary material” also includes a requirement: 1) if it is not practicable to copy a document or object (pursuant to in s42 (1) (c), (d) or (e) of the definition of evidentiary material) – to serve a notice that describes it and states where and when it can be inspected; (The fact that a large number of documents have been gathered in the course of the investigation does not mean, it is not practicable to copy the documents. It is confined to circumstances where it is not practicable to copy a particular document (i.e. a large map). 2) If a copy of a statement or recording of a person is served – to also provide a copy of any statement or recording of the person that is inconsistent with the statement or recording; 3) to serve notice of the name and address of any person from whom no statement or recording was taken but who the prosecutor thinks may be able to assist the accused’s defence and a description of the evidence concerned. PURPOSE

This document is a user reference to Endorsement to Charge (ETC)/Evidence Matrix (EM) procedures.

Supervisors are to refer to Section 2.3 of the Integrated Staged Brief Process Manual for a comprehensive guide to brief preparation.

This document applies to all offences investigated by the Western Australia Police Force (WA Police Force). This document is to be read in conjunction with the WA Police Investigation Doctrine.

The document is a quick reference to the ETC/EM procedure.

sections.h:
Where there are multiple offences of different jurisdictional responsibility each offence, from an ETC perspective, is to be dealt with on an individual basis and according to the following instructions.

Where there are multiple offences of such a nature that the similarities outweigh the differences then they may be captured on the same EM in accordance with the following instructions.

It is the Investigating Officer’s (IO) responsibility to ensure that only substantially similar offences with substantially similar evidence are grouped within the same ETC. However ultimate responsibility rests with the Endorsing Officer to ensure that they have received sufficient information (either verbally or in writing, dependant on court of jurisdiction) to substantiate prima facie on each individual charge.

sections.t: Offences Dealt with by Other Than A

sections.h:
This applies to offences that are dealt with by means other than charge including:

• Infringement;

• JJT referral; or

• Caution.

1. No Endorsement to Charge required when issuing the infringement.

2. If the accused elects to go to court.

a. Non-Criminal Code offences do not require an ETC; or b. Criminal Code offences do not require an ETC however an assessment of the evidence should be made to ensure there is sufficient to proceed to court.

Rationale

The process supports the introduction of infringements for criminal offences. NTIMS and CCIN support the reduction of seized property by WA Police. For NTIMS and CCIN matters police do not retain the property. The property is to be retained by the owner. Photographs of the property should be retained by the owner in case the offender elects to go to court.

sections.h:
This applies to any simple offence dealt with via the court system:

• In the case of Prosecution Notices the EM should be endorsed prior to advising the

accused of the intention to charge and prior to commencing bail.

• In the case of a Court Hearing Notice the EM should be endorsed prior to

commencing the brief.

An EM is required for all simple offences except those exempted.

The IO will populate the elements of the offence. They are not required to record the evidence in writing as it will be presented verbally to a supervisor.

The EM is presented to an endorsing officer who must be a Senior Constable or above.

The endorsing officer will:

• Initial each element if they are satisfied a prima facie exists;

• If considered necessary take notes to justify their decision; and

• Endorse the ETC with their final decision.

At this point there are two options:

1. If the charge is not endorsed the investigation should be filed or referred for

reinvestigation; or

2. If the charge is endorsed it should progress to brief.

Note: If the charge has not been endorsed and further evidence is being sought ensure the period of detention has not expired.

Rationale

The process streamlines the compilation of the EM and reduces the time taken to physically complete it. However it maintains the same rigour from a supervisory perspective and ensures that appropriate quality control triggers are in place.

It is the supervisors’ decision as to whether they will record notes to support their decisions.

sections.h:
This applies to any either way offence dealt with via the court system.

• In the case of Prosecution Notices the EM should be endorsed prior to advising the

accused of the intention to charge and prior to commencing bail.

• In the case of a Court Hearing Notice the EM should be endorsed prior to

commencing the brief.

An EM is required for all either way offences except those exempted.

The IO will populate the elements of the offence. They are not required to record the evidence in writing as these will be presented verbally to a supervisor.

The EM is presented to an endorsing officer who must be an Acting Sergeant or above.

The endorsing officer will:

• Initial each element if they are satisfied a prima facie exists;

• If considered necessary take notes to justify their decision; and

• Make a final decision.

At this point there are three options:

1. Endorse the EM;

2. Not endorse the EM and record the rationale. The investigation should be filed or

referred for reinvestigation; or

3. Not endorse the EM and request the completion of a written EM that is the same as

an EM compiled for a purely Indictable Offence.

Note: If the charge has not been endorsed and further evidence is being sought ensure the period of detention has not expired.

Rationale

The process streamlines the completion of the EM and reduces the time taken to physically complete it. However it maintains the same rigour from a supervisory perspective and ensures that appropriate quality control triggers are in place.

It is the supervisors’ decision as to whether they will record notes to support their decisions.

The scaling of the intrusive supervision into these more serious offences allows for the supervisor to request a full EM as a third option at the endorsing stage.

• In the case of Prosecution Notices the EM should be endorsed prior to advising the

accused of the intention to charge and prior to commencing bail.

• In the case of a Court Hearing Notice the EM should be endorsed prior to

commencing the brief.

EM is mandatory for all purely indictable offences.

Complete a full EM by populating the elements of the offence. The IO will record and map the prima facie evidence against the individual elements.

The EM is presented to an endorsing officer who must be an Acting Sergeant or above.

The endorsing officer will:

• Initial each element if they are satisfied a prima facie exists;

• May take notes to justify their decision; and

• Make a final decision.

At this point there are two options:

1. Endorse the EM; or

2. Not endorse the EM, note the reason and the investigation should be filed or

referred for further investigation.

Note: If the charge has not been endorsed and further evidence is being sought ensure the period of detention has not expired.

Rationale

The process ensures the most rigour is applied to the most serious offences. Whilst still maintaining appropriate intrusive supervision.

These exceptions have previously been ratified by the CIT and detailed in ‘From the Line’ in November 2013. Maintenance of these exceptions in the first instance will assist with the acceptance of the new policy.

The ETC process does not apply to the following:

• Stealing (under $500): Section 378 Criminal Code

• Disorderly Behaviour: Section 74A (2)(a) and (b) Criminal Code

• Breach of Bail: Section 51 Bail Act

• Fail to Obey Order: Section 153 (1) Criminal Investigation Act

The ETC process is not mandatory for any Road Traffic Act or Road Traffic Code offences except:

• Fail to Stop (Circumstance of Aggravation): Section 53 (2A)(b) Road Traffic Act;

• Driver in Incident Occasioning Bodily Harm to Stop, Ensure Assistance and Give

Information: Section 54 Road Traffic Act;

• Driver in Incident Occasioning Bodily Harm or Property Damage to Report Incident

to Police (Incident Occasioned Death or Grievous Bodily Harm): Section 56(2) Road Traffic Act;

• Dangerous Driving Causing Death or Grievous Bodily Harm: Section 59 Road Traffic

Act;

• Dangerous Driving Causing Bodily Harm: Section 59A Road Traffic Act;

• Reckless Driving: Section 60 Road Traffic Act; and

• Failure to Comply with Sect 66 Requirement to Provide Breath, Blood or Urine

Sample (commonly referred to as Jessie’s Law): Section 67 (3a) Road Traffic Act.

sections.h:
The ETC/EM are interchangeable terms.

The EM is a record of the evidence gathered and mapped against each element of an offence. Whether the evidence is presented verbally or in writing, supervisors must apply the same degree of scrutiny.

By endorsing an EM the supervisor gives approval to charge.

The supervisor is not required to read each statement and review the CRIME File. The decision to charge is to be based on the evidence presented. Supervisors are entitled to review the CRIME file at their discretion.

Elements: Ensure the correct elements for the designated offence have been included.

Evidence: Ensure there is prima facie for each of the elements. It is the supervisor’s responsibility to satisfy themselves that sufficient evidence exists to prefer a charge. The supervisor must record whatever notes deemed necessary to justify their decision.

Whether presented verbally or in written format the evidence need only be a summary and there is no need to outline every aspect of that piece of evidence.

Defences: If the submitting officer has raised a possible defence then it is prudent to discuss this further to ensure a prima facie case still exists. Just because a lawful defence has been raised it does not negate the fact a charge could still be preferred.

Endorsing Officers Comments: This section is to be completed by the endorsing officer and should reflect whether there is prima facie evidence for the elements presented or whether further investigation is required. Writing ‘endorsed’ is sufficient.

If you have written ‘not endorsed’ for any element the reasons need to be recorded and explained to the submitting officer. Advice should be provided to the submitting officer outlining possible further investigative actions to address this shortfall. All non-approved EM need to be retained as it forms part of any future disclosure brief.

Endorsement to Charge: This section is to be completed by the endorsing officer after evaluation of the evidence outlined in the matrix to establish whether a charge can be preferred or not.

sections.h:
Elements: In this column record the individual elements of the particular offence. It is important you do not combine two elements into one. Each individual element MUST be addressed. i.e. ‘Unlawfully’ and ‘assaults’ are two entirely separate elements and evidence needs to be supplied to establish a prima facie case for each element. The elements for most common offences can be located by accessing the WA Police Investigative Standards intranet site.

Evidence: The evidence is a dot point summary of the evidence available to establish a prima facie for each element of the charge. At the top of the column write the alleged fact related to the element you wish to establish and then articulate who will provide this evidence.

For example to establish prima facie evidence in relation to the element of ‘bodily harm’ for a charge of Assault Occasioning Causing Harm the following would meet this requirement:

“Evidence of LEACH (s) (paragraph 12) states the punch caused his injury, namely a fractured eye socket, interfering with his health and comfort.”

The (s) indicates that the submitting officer has a statement from the complainant/witness and (paragraph 12) shows this evidence is contained in paragraph 12 of the statement.

Multiple witnesses can give evidence for each element. It is not necessary to name every witness, it is only necessary to establish a prima facie case.

Defences: If a defence has been raised by the suspect or intimated during the course of the investigation this should be addressed by the submitting officer to determine if a prima facie case exists. Any other defences to the charge that exist in law should be discussed with your supervisor. If no defences are apparent then state ‘Nil defences identified or raised’

sections.h:
Any application made in a prosecution to a court of summary jurisdiction is governed by the Criminal Procedure Regulations 2005. Unless specifically excluded by the Regulations; and these applications being discussed are not; applications are to be made by way of a Form 6 Application in or after a prosecution (Form 6). The Magistrates Court also expects an accompanying affidavit to be lodged along with the Form 6 (r14(2) and (3)) This affidavit must contain sufficient evidence to satisfy the court that it is in the interests of justice to make the order prohibiting publication (suppression order) or to close the court. It must also state exactly what the order is being sought for.

Under r14(2)(c) there is the ability for a court, in a particular case, to permit an application to be made otherwise. In other words, a prosecutor, or defence counsel may, with the leave of the court, make an oral application from the bar table.

However, the appropriate course of action is to make the application by way of the Form 6 unless there are good reasons for not complying with the Regulations.

sections.h:
Where an investigating officer is of the opinion that a suppression order or an order to close the court should be sought at the first appearance, a Form 6 and accompanying affidavit in support can be lodged ‘along with the prosecution notice’. Whilst the prosecution notice is lodged electronically, the Form 6 can be physically lodged with the relevant court on the same day or a following day but prior to the court appearance date.

Where an accused is refused bail, the Form 6 can still be lodged ‘at the same time’.

The Form 6 and accompanying affidavit is also to be scanned and attached to the electronic prosecution brief as per normal for the information of the prosecutor.

sections.h:
Where there are good reasons for not making an application in accordance with the Regulations, there is a fill-in-a-form attached to the end of these guidelines that should be completed and brought to the prosecutor’s attention. It must, like the affidavit, be completed with sufficient evidence to satisfy the court that it is in the interests of justice to make the order prohibiting publication (suppression order) or to close the court.

It also is to be uploaded and attached to the electronic prosecution brief.

An affidavit can be attached to the form instead of completing the grounds area if preferred.

An advantage with the affidavit is that it can possibly be tendered to the court and would carry more weight.
