sections.h:
An offender will often give police a false name in the hope of escaping justice. Quite often, this deception is discovered before any charge is laid. However, some accused are charged under the false name and some actually attend court and plead guilty under the false name or submit a written plea in the false name. In cases where the false name used is that of another actual, real, innocent person, the use of the false name can have some very inconvenient consequences for the innocent person if not corrected.

The correction, however, is a relatively simple process. Section 179 was specifically included in the Criminal Procedure Act 2004 to address this problem.

“179. Errors in court records due to use of wrong or false name

(1) If as a result of an accused using a false name, address or date of birth, a record of a court does not

record the accused’s correct name, address or date of birth, the court may correct the record and make any ancillary orders needed to give effect to the correction.

(2) The powers in subsection (1) may be exercised by the court on its own initiative or on an application

by a person who the court is satisfied has a proper interest in the proceedings.

(3) Without limiting the power to make ancillary orders, the court may —

(a) set aside a conviction or order made by the court;

(b) order a new trial;

(c) order that records other than the court’s records be corrected.”

Prior to the Criminal Procedure Act 2004, an innocent person had to take the remedial action themselves and apply to the court to have the conviction quashed. Section 179 was enacted specifically to overcome this as stated by the Attorney General in the second reading speech:

“Tenth, the Criminal Procedure Bill provides for the correction of errors caused by the use of false names. Difficulties have been experienced for many years by courts and police in trying to assist individuals who have convictions recorded against their names for offences they did not commit. These convictions have occurred as a result of other individuals using their names when apprehended, for example, for offences under the Road Traffic Act 1974. The only option available currently is an application by the innocent person to the court to have the matter re-heard and the conviction quashed. The new provision will rectify the current unsatisfactory situation, which requires the affected innocent party to rectify an error that was caused through no fault of their own.”1

When an offender is charged, they become an accused. If the accused gives a false name and a charge is laid and prosecution commenced under that false name, it is still the accused who has been charged, irrespective of the name given. If the false name is that of another, real person that actually exists, it is still the accused that has been charged, not the other person.

There is no need to discontinue the charge.

1 MR J.A. McGinty (Fremantle - Attorney General) Criminal Procedure Bill 2004, 2nd Reading Speech, Assembly, 26 August 2004.

A problem arises, however, where investigating police, upon finding out the accused has used a false name, charge the accused with new charges such as ‘use false name’ but then again charge the accused with the original charge. It is arguable that the fresh original charge is subject to double jeopardy, especially where a finding has been entered. Perhaps the safest solution for a prosecutor is to apply to the court to correct the original prosecution notice to reflect the correct name and then discontinue the second identical ‘fresh’ charge.

However, a prosecutor needs to have both charges before the court to do this.

Prosecutors also need to be mindful of the statute of limitation in regards to simple offences.

Example - An accused is to be charged with DUI and gives their brother’s name. They are subsequently convicted, fined and disqualified. They get sentenced as a first offender when in actual fact this is their third time and they face a permanent disqualification. It does not come to light until some 12 months later when the brother becomes aware of it. If the charge was to be brought before the court and the conviction overturned and the charge subsequently discontinued, the accused will not be able to be recharged because of the 12 months limitation to commence a prosecution.

However, if an application under section179 Criminal Procedure Act 2004 is made, the details of the conviction and disqualification will simply be amended to reflect the accused’s details. The prosecution was commenced within the 12 months limitation; indeed, it was finalised within the 12 months. It is simply the error in the recording of the matter that is being fixed or adjusted.

If, as in the above example, the use of a false name has also resulted in an incorrect sentence being handed down originally, then the police prosecutor can invite the court to recall the original sentencing order and resentence correctly. So, in the above case, the court could amend the name and also recall or set aside the sentence and resentence the accused as a subsequent, third time offender. (This is addressed more fully in the module on sentencing.)

Section 179(3) also allows the court to set aside the conviction, although, generally, this should not be required.

Expand the above to where an accused commits multiple simple offences over a period of time and gives his brother’s name because the brother lives overseas. Again, an application under section 179 should suffice to have the matters corrected.

Where an accused is sentenced under a false name and receives a period of disqualification under a road law, they still commit the offence of driving contrary to the disqualification even though the disqualification is under the false name.

In 1993, an appeal decision clearly confirmed that. In that case, an offender was charged with driving with a blood alcohol in excess of 0.08% and gave his brother’s name. He attended court, pleaded guilty and was fined and disqualified for three months. It was later found that he had used his brother’s name. He was subsequently caught driving and charged with driving under suspension (under his correct name). The original 0.08 charge and findings had not been reheard or amended in any way.

In the appeal decision, Scott J stated -

“The more important question is whether the conviction for driving with a blood alcohol in excess of 0.08% was good as against the respondent.”2 He further found that -

“…it is clear that by whatever name he was then known, it was the respondent whose motor driver's licence was suspended on 31 March 1993.”3 (Emphasis added.) This decision further enforces why the original charge in the false name should not be discontinued but simply be corrected. If the original conviction is set aside, the disqualification is deemed to have not existed so any alleged breach would consequently cease to exist.

When lodging an application under s179 of the Criminal Procedure Act 2004, there is no requirement to serve the accused with a copy and the application can be made in their absence.

Criminal Procedure Regulations 2005

“29. Correction of court record, application for (Act s. 179)

(1) If an application made under the CPA section 179 to correct a record of a court in respect of an

accused is made by or on behalf of the prosecutor in the relevant prosecution against the accused, the application —

(a) need not be served on the accused; and

(b) may be dealt with in the absence of the accused, unless a court orders otherwise.

(2) If an application made under the CPA section 179 to correct a record of a court in a prosecution is

made by a person other than the prosecutor, the application —

(a) must be served on the prosecutor; and

(b) must not be dealt with in the absence of the prosecutor unless a court considers it is in the

interests of justice to do so.”

Consequently, if a situation arises where an accused has used a false name and a subsequent conviction is adversely impacting upon another innocent person, an application can be made to correct the court records even if the accused is not present, or perhaps is avoiding police.

However, the prosecutor will need to be able to satisfy the court of the reasons why the change should be made; for example, fingerprints or DNA might have revealed the deception. Usually, the investigating officer will have the information needed.

2 Keady v Bradford [1993] WASC Lib. No. 930731, Scott J at page 7.

3 Keady v Bradford [1993] WASC Lib. No. 930731, Scott J at page 8.

sections.b: When an offender is charged, they become an accused. If the accused gives a false name and the charge is laid and prosecution commenced under that false name, it is still the accused who has been charged, irrespective of the name given. If the false name is that of another, real person that actually exists, it is still the accused that has been charged, not the other person. Section 179 of the Criminal Procedure Act 2004 was specifically enacted to address this problem. The Act allows the name to be corrected. There is no need to discontinue and recharge. sections.h:
WHAT IS A NON-DISCLOSURE ORDER?

Police are required under the Criminal Procedure Act 2004 (the Act) to disclose all confessional and evidentiary material in our possession to the accused. The requirements are contained within sections 35, 42, 61 and 95 of the Act. However, under section 138 of the Act, the court may make an order that dispenses with part or all of a disclosure requirement.

The order will be limited to very specific evidence.

“138. Disclosure requirements, orders as to

(1) In this section, unless the contrary intention appears —

disclosure requirement means a requirement under section 35, 42, 61, 62, 95 or 96 to disclose material, other than a requirement under section 62(4)(a) or 96(3)(a).

(2) The powers in this section may be exercised by a court on its own initiative or on an application by a

party to a case.

(3) A court may, in respect of a disclosure requirement, make an order —

(a) that dispenses with all or part of the requirement, if it is satisfied —

(i) there is a good reason to do so; and

(ii) no miscarriage of justice will result;

or

(b) that shortens or extends the time for obeying the requirement; or

(c) that amends or cancels an order made previously under this section, whether by the court or

some other court; or

(d) as to any other matter that the court considers is just.

(4) An application for an order under this section may be made by a prosecutor without notice to the

accused and may be dealt with in the absence of the accused.

(4a) Despite section 171, an application by a prosecutor for an order under this section that is made

without notice to the accused must not be dealt with in open court and the only people who may be present when it is dealt with are the applicant and those permitted by the court.

(5) If an order is made under this section in the absence of an accused, the order must not be given or

disclosed to the accused without the permission of the court that made it or, if it was made by a court of summary jurisdiction and the accused is committed for trial or sentence to a superior court, of the superior court.

[Section 138 amended by No. 5 of 2008 s. 46.]”

A non-disclosure order does not prevent disclosure of the evidence if circumstances change.

Nor does the order prevent the defence from trying to subpoena the evidence if they become aware of it. (This has been clarified and confirmed with the Chief Magistrate.) Note, however, that under subsection (5), if the application is made in the absence of the accused, the order must not be given or disclosed to the accused.

There must be a good reason for making a non-disclosure application and an investigating officer must ensure that no miscarriage of justice will result.

There tends to be two main reasons for seeking a non-disclosure order in the absence of the accused. One is where the police want a delay in the disclosure of the evidence until closer

to the trial. This may be for the protection of a witness or to enable an operation to continue, etc.

For example: a witness may have supplied a statement to police and will be required to give evidence but if the accused became aware of the witness they would be in danger. The police may be arranging witness protection but it might not be available until closer to the trial. A delay in the disclosure would enable the witness’ safety to be ensured.

Another example could be where legal phone taps are still in place and disclosure would alert the accused.

The second reason is where the evidence would have no bearing on the accused’s defence and the police do not want the accused to know about it at all. The evidence would not be used at trial.

For example, police may have a major operation going and are sitting off the house of a target. Along comes little Billy Brownpants and buys a small amount of cannabis from the target. Billy is followed and a traffic stop conducted and, lo and behold, he is found in possession of cannabis. The observations, etc, at the house, if disclosed, could easily tip off the target. So, police would not want that specific evidence being disclosed and it would not be used in court.

The order given is a standard order stating the “requirements of Section 35, 42 or 61 of the Criminal Procedure Act 2004 have been dispensed with in relation to the material” which has been clearly described in the applicant’s confidential affidavit. Indeed, the one order can cover material that the applicant will disclose later but before trial and other material that the applicant does not to intend to disclose at all.

As stated before, the order does not prevent material that is to be disclosed later from being disclosed earlier than planned nor does it prevent material which was not intended to be disclosed at all from being disclosed should circumstances change.

Simply disclose it.

Some matters need not be disclosed under the law of public interest immunity.1 There is sometimes confusion as to when a non-disclosure order should be obtained rather than using the law of public interest immunity.

Very simplistically it can be divided up as follows:

• Where the defence has no idea the material exists, then get a non-disclosure order.

• Where the defence does or should know of the existence of the material, use the law of

public interest immunity.

1 Criminal Procedure Act 2004 section 137A.

Let us use a person charged with ‘possess prohibited drug with intent to sell/supply’. An undercover police officer (UCO) speaks to the offender during an operation and conducts a controlled buy which is not intended to be used as evidence and further ascertains that the offender has a quantity of drugs at their home which they intend to sell. Investigating police take out a Misuse of Drugs Act 1981 search warrant and ‘tip’ the house.

The defence would not be aware of the UCO and to disclose that information about the UCO and the controlled buy would jeopardise the operation. A non-disclosure order could be obtained for that material directly relating to the UCO and the controlled buy.

When taking out the search warrant, though, police would need to complete an ‘information on oath for search warrant’ form. As this is required by law and the form is in the Misuse of Drugs Regulations 1982, defence would be aware of its existence. In this case, the law of public interest immunity would apply to not disclosing all or part of it, especially in regards to the UCO and their information.

APPLICATIONS

An application should be made as early as possible before the trial or disclosure/committal hearing date to enable the investigating officer to comply with their legislative disclosure requirements.

Applications for matters that are appearing in the Magistrates Court are made without notice to the accused and are heard in the absence of the accused.

All applications, for all matters statewide, are made in Perth* and any order that is granted will be granted by the Chief or Deputy Chief Magistrate. Any order will only relieve the requirements under sections 35, 42 or 61. These will, generally, be for summary trials and matters going to a disclosure/committal hearing.

These applications are dealt with by a dedicated prosecutor from the Prosecution Standards And Capability.

Once a matter has been committed up to the District or Supreme Court, the DPP will need to make another application before that court to be relieved of any requirement under section 95 of the Act. These orders are granted in the relevant superior court.

Non-disclosure for Children's Court matters should be referred to the Children's Court DPP team at Perth Children's Court.

The required forms for making a non-disclosure application, along with this document and an Applicant’s Checklist, are located on the Prosecution Division’s Blue Book site (Blue Book | Strategy and Legal - Non-Disclosure Documents - All Documents (sharepoint.com)). The forms can also be accessed through the Prosecution System in Forms and Printing > Additional Forms.

NOTE: The confidential affidavit and Form 6 is to be signed in front of the Chief or Deputy Chief Magistrate unless the applicant is in regional WA and not able to attend.

For a fuller description of non-disclosure, refer to the Disclosure Manual.

*Regulation 46A Magistrates Court (General) Rules 2005

"46A. Disclosure requirements, applications relating to (CPA s. 138) An application made by a prosecutor without notice to the accused for an order under the CPA section 138 must be heardby a magistrate sitting in chambers at the Court sitting at Perth.

[Rule 46A inserted in Gazette 24 Aug 2007 p. 4323; amended in Gazette 23 Nov 2007 p. 5862.]"
